pathophysiology_summary: Lichen planus is a T-cell-mediated inflammatory dermatosis producing pruritic, violaceous, flat-topped (polygonal) papules with fine white surface lines (Wickham striae), classically on the flexor wrists and ankles, resolving with post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. It is non-contagious. The intense itch and papular eruption commonly raise fear of scabies; the discriminators are the characteristic morphology and distribution, the presence of Wickham striae, and the absence of finger web-space burrows and affected contacts. Confirmed clinically (± biopsy) and treated with potent topical corticosteroids and antihistamines, the acute telehealth task is itch relief, reassurance of non-contagiousness, in-person confirmation, and a secondary-infection safety-net for the excoriated skin.

differential.evidence_basis: Violaceous flat-topped papules with Wickham striae on flexor wrists/ankles.
Intense nocturnal itch prompts consideration; excluded by absent web-space burrows/contacts and the distinct morphology.
Discrete papules rather than confluent eczematous plaques and no exposure history.
Screen excoriated skin for infection; absent here.
Characteristic morphology with Wickham striae, no burrows/contacts (DI-001), no secondary infection (DI-002), no new exposures or mucosal lesions (DI-003) — cutaneous lichen planus.
No web-space burrows and no itchy contacts; morphology is not scabetic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: reassure non-contagious, potent topical corticosteroid + sedating antihistamine at night, arrange in-person confirmation, and safety-net for secondary infection. Household scabies treatment or ED escalation is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection of excoriated skin (spreading warmth/redness, pus, fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Excoriated skin can become secondarily infected.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated infection can progress to cellulitis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Presumptively treating as scabies and instituting household treatment without noting the lichen planus morphology and absent burrows/contacts.
Failing to safety-net the raw, excoriated skin.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Skin biopsy to confirm lichen planus
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: In-person GP/dermatology confirmation; potent topical steroid + night antihistamine; secondary-infection safety-net.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Mislabelling as scabies causes unnecessary household treatment; missing a secondary infection risks cellulitis.
